title: Yuki-Onna: The Snow Woman
summary: A snow spirit spares a young man's life on one condition: that he never reveal the secret of their encounter, a promise that will one day be tested by love.
full_text:
In a remote village of Musashi Province, there lived two woodcutters: Mosaku, a weathered old man, and Minokichi, his young and spirited apprentice. One winter evening, as they made their way home through the cedar forests, a terrible snowstorm overtook them—a white wall of ice and wind that erased the path and blinded their eyes. They sought shelter in a small, abandoned hut by the river, where only two mats of space remained and no fire burned to ward off the creeping frost.

During the night, Minokichi awoke to feel a single, freezing flake of snow falling on his face, despite being indoors. By the pale, spectral light streaming through the door, he saw a woman in shimmering white, bending over the sleeping Mosaku. Her skin was as white as the drifts outside, and her eyes held a cold, distant beauty. She leaned down and breathed upon the old man—and Mosaku froze instantly, his breath turning to ice, his face becoming a mask of white snow.

The woman turned to Minokichi. She studied his young, terrified face, and for a moment, a flicker of something like compassion crossed her frozen features. She smiled softly, a gesture as cold as the wind. 'I intended to treat you as I treated the other,' she whispered, her voice a chilling melody. 'But I cannot hurt you because you are so young and beautiful. However, if you ever tell anyone—not even your mother—about what you have seen tonight, I will find you and kill you.' Then she dissolved into the storm, her form blending with the swirling white until she was gone.

When dawn broke, Minokichi found Mosaku frozen solid, a statue of ice in the same position he had slept. The boy returned home, but he carried the secret like a heavy stone in his chest, never speaking a word of the encounter for many years.

The next winter, while walking through the village, Minokichi met a beautiful girl named O-Yuki. Her skin was impossibly pale, her hair a river of obsidian, and her presence brought a strange, cooling calm to those around her. They fell deeply in love, and with his mother's blessing, they married. O-Yuki became a devoted wife and bore Minokichi ten children—boys and girls—and for years, their home was filled with a happiness that seemed to ward off the winter cold.

But one winter night, after the children had gone to bed, O-Yuki suddenly stood, her eyes glowing with a faint, icy light. 'I must tell you something,' she said, her voice returning to that chilling melody from the hut. 'I am the woman you met in the snow all those years ago. I told you I would kill you if you ever spoke of that night—but I cannot kill you now, for I love you, and I love our children. Take care of them, for I can no longer stay in this world of warmth.' She walked to the door, and in an instant, she melted away into the falling snow, leaving behind only a cold draft and a heart shattered by a love that was never meant to last.
moral: Love can bridge the gap between worlds, but the truth, once revealed, can destroy the most fragile of bonds.

title: Yara-ma-yha-who: The Little Red Men
summary: In the old times, children were told: 'Do not wander alone in the bush.. Do not rest beneath the fig trees.
full_text:
In the old times, children were told: 'Do not wander alone in the bush. Do not rest beneath the fig trees. This was not just advice—it was warning. The Yara-ma-yha-who were waiting.

They were small—no taller than a child of ten. Their skin was red, like dried blood. Their heads were too large for their bodies, their mouths were too wide, and they had no teeth. They lived in the wild fig trees, hiding among the leaves.

The Yara-ma-yha-who did not hunt. They were too patient for hunting. They waited—silent, still—until someone came beneath their tree. A traveler, perhaps, stopping to rest. A child, wandering alone.

When a victim stopped beneath the fig tree, the Yara-ma-yha-who would drop—silently, like a shadow. They would land on the person, their hands and feet finding the flesh. And they had suckers—like octopuses—on their fingers and toes.

They would drain the blood, not killing—it was not their way. They would drink until the victim grew weak. Then they would swallow the person whole, and go to sleep in a comfortable place.

When they woke, they would regurgitate the victim—but the person was changed. Smaller. Weaker. And red—their skin was stained red. They would be sent home, and within a year—or less—they would be back in the fig tree, looking for someone else.

Those who saw a Yara-ma-yha-who and lived were never the same. They carried the curse: every attack, they became a little smaller, a little more like the little red men.

The lesson: some dangers come to those who wander. Stay on the path. Stay with others. Do not rest where you are not welcome.

Children were told: behave, or the Yara-ma-yha-who will come. And they believed—and they were safe.
moral: Wander not alone—danger waits in unexpected places.

title: Ysatters-Kajsa, the Wind-Troll of Narke
summary: A Swedish legend about Ysatters-Kajsa, a powerful troll-woman who controls the winds over Narke province. She creates storms and blizzards when angered.
full_text:
In the province of Narke in central Sweden, where the forests stretch endlessly and the lakes lie like mirrors beneath the gray Scandinavian sky, there lived a troll-woman of such fearsome power that her name was whispered only in the safety of well-lit rooms. She was called Ysatters-Kajsa, and she ruled the winds.

Ysatters-Kajsa was no ordinary troll. She was ancient beyond reckoning, born in the time when the great glaciers carved the fjords and mountains of Scandinavia. She had lived in the wildest, most desolate parts of Narke for centuries, dwelling in a cave high on Mount Kilsbergen, where the wind never stopped howling. Her hair was the color of frost, her skin like cracked ice, and her eyes were the pale blue of a winter sky moments before a blizzard.

Her power was simple but absolute: she controlled the wind. When she was pleased, she sent gentle breezes to turn the millers' wheels and fill the sailors' sails. When she was angered, she summoned storms that could tear roofs from houses and sink ships on Lake Hjalmaren. And when she was truly enraged, she called forth the ys, the fierce winter wind that blasted across Narke with such force that it stripped the bark from trees and turned the land into a frozen wasteland.

The people of Narke learned to live with Ysatters-Kajsa. They left offerings of milk and bread on their doorsteps. They never spoke ill of her, and they taught their children to respect the wind as a living thing. When the wind blew gently, they thanked Kajsa. When it howled, they stayed indoors and waited for her mood to pass.

One year, a young farmer named Lars moved to Narke from the southern province of Skane. He was practical and skeptical, and he laughed at the old stories. "A troll who controls the wind?" he scoffed. "That is just superstitious nonsense. The wind is the wind, nothing more."

His neighbors warned him, but Lars ignored them. He built his farm on the exposed slopes of Kilsbergen, right in the path of the mountain winds. He cut down the ancient trees that had stood for centuries, ignoring the whispers that these were Kajsa's trees. He plowed fields where the wind was meant to blow freely.

Ysatters-Kajsa watched from her cave. At first, she merely sent stronger breezes, trying to warn the young man. The wind rattled his windows and tugged at his hat. Lars shook his fist at the sky. "Is that all you have, old woman?" he shouted.

That was a mistake.

Ysatters-Kajsa rose from her cave, her frost-white hair streaming behind her. She raised her arms, and the wind began to build. It started as a low moan, then grew to a shriek, then to a roar that shook the very mountains. The sky turned black. The temperature plummeted. Snow began to fall, not in flakes but in a blinding white wall.

For three days and three nights, the ys raged across Narke. Lars's farm was buried under ten feet of snow. His roof collapsed. His livestock froze. When the storm finally subsided, Lars emerged to find his farm destroyed, his crops ruined, and his animals dead. He finally understood the power he had mocked.

He climbed the mountain to Kajsa's cave, shivering in his thin coat. He fell to his knees in the snow. "I was wrong!" he cried. "I beg your forgiveness!"

Ysatters-Kajsa appeared at the mouth of her cave. Her eyes were no longer angry but weary. "You humans are so young, so foolish," she said. "You think you can defy the forces that shaped this land. But the wind was here before you, and it will be here after you are gone. You must learn to live with it, not fight against it."

"What must I do?" Lars asked.

"Rebuild your farm in the valley, where the wind is gentler. Plant trees to break the force of the storms. And remember: the wind is not your enemy. It is a force of nature, and nature must be respected."

Lars did as she advised. He rebuilt in the valley, planted a windbreak of pines, and lived in peace with the land. He became one of the farmers who left offerings for Ysatters-Kajsa, and in return, the winds of Narke were kind to him.

Ysatters-Kajsa still lives in her cave on Kilsbergen. Or so the people of Narke say. On winter nights, when the wind howls around the houses and the snow beats against the windows, they remind each other to speak respectfully of the wind-troll. For Ysatters-Kajsa is always listening, and her power is as vast and wild as the Scandinavian sky itself.

The legend serves as a reminder that nature is not to be conquered but to be respected, and that those who mock the old powers do so at their peril.
moral: Do not anger those who control the forces of nature - the wind-troll's storms are terrible.

title: Yuletide Specters
summary: A woman riding to Christmas mass encounters witch-trolls at a bridge and finds the church filled with headless specters and a headless priest, barely escaping with scraps of her mantle scattered on every grave. This ghostly Christmas legend is common throughout Scandinavia.
moral: Faith and the sign of the cross protect even in the most haunted of places.
source: The Swedish Fairy Book, ed. Clara Stroebe (1921), Ch.V: Yuletide Specters. From an mss. communicated by Dr. v. Sydow-Lund. The tale of the weird service of the dead on Christmas night is common throughout Scandinavia.

title: Yanauluha Brings Medicine and Agriculture
summary: In Zuni mythology, Yanauluha is the benevolent culture hero who brought the arts of medicine and agriculture to the people. Unlike trickster figures in some traditions, Yanauluha is a dignified, wise teacher. After the emergence into the daylight world, the people were struggling to survive. Yanauluha appeared among them carrying seeds, digging sticks, and sacred objects. He taught them which plants were edible and medicinal, how to plant corn, beans, and squash together in hills, when to plant by observing the stars and the position of the sun, and how to prepare and store food. He also taught them how to use plants for healing — which roots, leaves, and barks cured various illnesses. Yanauluha established the pattern of the agricultural year and the ceremonies that accompany planting and harvest. He is associated with the directions and with the sacred mountains that surround the Zuni world. After teaching the people, Yanauluha departed, but his knowledge continues through Zuni farmers and medicine people.
source: Frank Cushing, Outlines of Zuni Creation Myths (1896); various Zuni oral traditions

title: Yunwi Tsunsdi: The Little People (Cherokee)
summary: Yunwi Tsunsdi are the Cherokee Little People — small, child-sized nature spirits who live in caves, under rocks, and in the dense forest. They are usually invisible but sometimes reveal themselves to humans they trust. They possess powerful magic and are said to have taught medicine to certain shamans. The Little People can be helpful to those who respect them, leading lost hunters to safety or granting blessings. However, they are easily angered by disrespect and punish those who bother them excessively with confusion or misfortune. They are known to borrow tools from villagers, always returning them with small gifts of thanks. The Yunwi Tsunsdi are not to be spoken of lightly; those who encounter them rarely discuss the experience directly.
source: Wikipedia - Cherokee spiritual beliefs; native-languages.org Yunwi Tsunsdi

title: Yemoja and the Waters of Life (Yoruba)
summary: Yemoja, the mother of all waters and the goddess of the Ogun River, is said to be the mother of many of the Orishas. According to legend, when her waters burst forth, they created the oceans and rivers of the world. Yemoja is the patroness of pregnancy, childbirth, and all nurturing aspects of womanhood. In one tale, when the world was threatened by drought, Yemoja emerged from the depths carrying the moon in her womb, bringing the tides and the rains. Women pray to her for fertility and safe delivery. Her devotees dress in blue and white and offer her rice, fish, and gourds. She represents the maternal essence of the universe, the primordial ocean from which all life emerged, and her name literally means 'Mother whose children are the fish.'

title: Yewe, the God of Thunder (Ewe)
summary: Yewe (also known as So or Hevioso) is the Ewe god of thunder, lightning, and rain. Yewe is a trɔwo (lesser deity) descended from Mawu, and is considered one of the most powerful and feared spirits in the Ewe pantheon. Those who offend Yewe may be struck by lightning, and special priests (Yewe priests) are needed to intercede. The Yewe cult has elaborate initiation ceremonies in which new initiates spend months in seclusion learning sacred songs, dances, and rituals. The sacred drumming tradition called Sowu is performed exclusively for Yewe ceremonies. Yewe's sacred color is red, and his shrine typically holds a carved wooden figure holding an axe, reminiscent of the Yoruba Shango. During annual festivals, Yewe's priests dance in possession trance, channeling the god's power for the community's blessing.

title: Yamal Creation from the Mammoth
summary: A Nenets creation myth from the Yamal Peninsula, whose name means 'End of the Land' in the Nenets language. The legend tells that the world was formed from the body of a giant mammoth that emerged from the primordial sea. The great sky god Num sent the mammoth to create dry land for humans and reindeer. When the mammoth died, its massive body became the Yamal Peninsula, its bones became the Ural Mountains, and its great tusks formed the rivers. Ice spirits (Yul-Chyn) emerged from the mammoth's frozen breath. The Nenets traditionally believed that earthquakes were caused by the mammoth stirring beneath the permafrost. This myth explains the origin of frozen mammoth carcasses that periodically emerge from melting tundra.

title: Yemayá, Mother of the Ocean
summary: Yemayá (Yemoja) is the great mother orisha of the ocean, maternity, and the moon in Cuban Santería, syncretized with the Virgin of Regla. She is the goddess of living waters, the salt sea, and is considered the mother of all life and the protector of women and children. Her colors are blue and white, her number is 7, and her symbol is a fan. A famous patakí tells how Yemayá gave her long magical hair to her sister Oshún as a gift of generosity, binding their realms of salt and fresh water forever. Yemayá is depicted as a mermaid or a beautiful woman emerging from the waves. She represents the maternal, protective, and sometimes fierce aspect of the divine feminine. Her domain is the ocean's depth and mystery.
source: Wikipedia; Center for Folklore Studies (Ohio State); Mythlok
moral: Not all that seems beautiful is safe
